title: SSC CGL T-I GA Section Test

questions.question: Which of the following statements best describes the book ‘The Elephant Whisperers’ by Bomman and Bellie?
options: A scientific study on elephant behaviour
A wildlife conservation memoir set in South India
A fictional story based on tribal folklore
An academic work on forest ecology
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (B) A wildlife conservation memoir set in South India Explanation: • Bomman and Bellie are the real-life protagonists of the Oscar-winning documentary 'The Elephant Whisperers'. • Subsequent books or memoirs written by or about them focus on their experiences as indigenous caretakers of orphaned elephants in the Mudumalai Tiger Reserve, Tamil Nadu. Information Booster: • 'The Elephant Whisperers' won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Short Subwect in 2023. • The story highlights the deep bond between humans and animals in the Kattunayakan tribal community. Additional Knowledge: • Mudumalai is located at the tri-wunction of Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, and Kerala.

questions.question: Which constitutional features in India are linked to the Australian Constitution? A) Freedom of trade and commerce B) Joint sitting of the two Houses C) Federal structure with strong Centre D) Advisory wurisdiction of the Supreme Court
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: (b) A and B both Explanation: · From the Australian Constitution, India borrowed: · Freedom of trade and commerce (Article 301–307). · Provision for Joint Sitting of both Houses of Parliament (Article 108). · Other features like a strong Centre came from Canada, and Advisory wurisdiction of the Supreme Court came from the Government of India Act, 1935. Information Booster: · India’s Constitution borrowed provisions from many countries. · USA → Fundamental Rights, Judicial Review. · UK → Parliamentary system, Rule of Law. · Canada → Federal structure with strong Centre. · Ireland → DPSPs, Nominated members in Rawya Sabha. · Australia → Trade & Commerce freedom, Joint Sitting. Additional Knowledge: · Option A (Freedom of trade & commerce) → From Australia (correct). · Option B (Joint sitting of Houses) → From Australia (correct). · Option C (Federal structure with strong Centre) → From Canada. · Option D (Advisory wurisdiction of SC) → From Government of India Act, 1935.

questions.question: In which of the following folk dances of South India do the dancers balance pots on their heads?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (C) Karagattam Explanation: • Karagattam is a popular folk dance of Tamil Nadu in which dancers balance beautifully decorated pots (karagam) on their heads while performing rhythmic movements. • The dance is traditionally performed in honour of the rain goddess Mariamman, seeking her blessings for good rainfall and prosperity. • It combines graceful footwork, balance, music, and acrobatic skills. Types of Karagattam: • Aatta Karagam – Performed for entertainment • Sakthi Karagam – Performed in temples as a ritual Information Booster: • Kolattam – Stick dance from Andhra Pradesh & Tamil Nadu • Mayilattam – Peacock dance from Tamil Nadu (performers dress like peacocks) • Pampu Attam – Snake dance from Tamil Nadu in honour of snake gods • Karagattam performers often place multiple pots or dance on narrow surfaces, showcasing skill and balance. • Usually accompanied by instruments like thavil and nadaswaram.

questions.question: In 1968, late Indian composer Pandit Ravi Shankar became the very first Indian to win the Grammy Award for which of the following music albums in the ‘Best Chamber Music Performance’ category?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: (b) Explanation: • In 1968, Pandit Ravi Shankar won his first Grammy Award. • The award was in the category Best Chamber Music Performance. • He won it for the album "West Meets East", created with violinist Yehudi Menuhin. Information Booster: • Pandit Ravi Shankar was one of the greatest sitar maestros of India. • He played a mawor role in popularizing Indian classical music in the West. • He was awarded Bharat Ratna in 1999. • His music influenced members of The Beatles, especially George Harrison. Additional Knowledge: • (a) The Living Room Sessions – Released much later in 2012; not related to the 1968 Grammy. • (c) The Concert for Bangladesh – Ravi Shankar performed in this event (1971), but the Grammy was shared for Album of the Year, not Chamber Music. • (d) Full Circle: Carnegie Hall 2000 – Won a Grammy in 2002 in the Best World Music Album category, much later.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is/are correct in the context of the religious development during the Gupta Empire? I. Nalanda University was founded by Kumargupta I of the Gupta dynasty in the 5th century CE. II. Narasimhagupta Baladitya was influenced by Mahayana Buddhism.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is:(b)Both I and II Explanation: • Statement I: Nalanda University was indeed founded by Kumargupta I in the 5th century CE, making it a leading center for Buddhist and secular education. • Statement II: Narasimhagupta Baladitya, the Gupta ruler, supported and was influenced by Mahayana Buddhism, which saw significant growth during his reign. Information Booster: • ​Mahayana Buddhism became an important sect in India and spread to China, Central Asia, and Japan, due to royal patronage during the Gupta Empire.​ • ​Nalanda University was recognized for its library, Mahavihara, and its contribution to the intellectual growth of India and the world.​ • Gupta rulers promoted both Hinduism and Buddhism, contributing to a religiously tolerant society. • The Gupta period is considered a golden age for art, literature, and science.

questions.question: Match the following. Indus Valley Civilisation site Excavator Indus Valley Site — Excavator (A) Harappa — (I) R Rao (B) Dholavira — (II) JP Joshi (C) Surkotada — (III) RS Bisht (D) Lothal — (IV) Daya Ram Sahini
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: (d) A-IV; B-III; C-II; D-I Explanation: · Harappa was excavated by Daya Ram Sahni in 1921. · Dholavira was excavated by RS Bisht, who uncovered its unique water-management system. · Surkotada was excavated by JP Joshi, who identified horse remains at the site. · Lothal was excavated by R Rao, famous for its dockyard discovery. Information Booster: · All four are mawor Indus Valley Civilization sites known for planned urban layout, drainage, and specialized structures like granaries and dockyards. · Dholavira and Lothal are especially known for trade-related structures.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is/are correct? Some metals can be beaten into thin sheets. This property is called malleability. Metals can be drawn into thin wires. Metals are good conductors of heat.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (C) 1, 2 and 3. Explanation: All three statements are correct: • Malleability refers to the ability of metals to be beaten into thin sheets, a characteristic property of many metals like gold and aluminum. • Ductility is the property of metals to be drawn into thin wires, seen in metals like copper and silver. • Conductivity is the ability of metals to conduct heat and electricity, which is why metals like copper and aluminum are widely used in electrical wiring and cooking utensils. Information Booster: • Malleability: The ability of metals to be hammered or rolled into thin sheets without breaking. This property is critical in the manufacturing of materials like aluminum foil and metal plates. • Ductility: The ability to be stretched into a wire. This is used in making electrical wires, cables, and steel for construction. • Thermal conductivity: Metals have a high thermal conductivity, which means they transfer heat efficiently. This is why metal pans are commonly used in cooking as they heat up quickly and distribute heat evenly. • Gold: Known for its extreme malleability and is used in creating thin layers (e.g., gold leaf). • Copper: Exhibits excellent ductility and is used extensively in electrical wiring. • Aluminum: A lightweight metal that is both malleable and ductile, widely used in industries like aerospace and packaging.

questions.question: With which of the following states is the tribal festival named 'Samakka Saarakka Jaathara' traditionally associated?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (D) Telangana Explanation: • Samakka–Sarakka Jatara, also known as Medaram Jatara, is one of the largest tribal festivals in India, traditionally celebrated in the state of Telangana. • It is held every two years (biennially) at Medaram village in the Mulugu district. • The festival honors the tribal goddesses Samakka and Sarakka (Saralamma), revered by the Koya tribal community. Information Booster: • Medaram Jatara is often called “Kumbh Mela of the Tribals.” • It attracts over 1 crore devotees, making it the largest tribal gathering in Asia. • The festival is organized by the Telangana State government along with tribal welfare departments. • It celebrates resistance and bravery shown by Samakka and Sarakka against the Kakatiya rulers.

questions.question: Which cellular structure controls cell motility, adhesion, and polarity at interphase, and facilitates the organisation of spindle poles during mitosis?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (b) Centrosomes. · Centrosomes are key cellular structures involved in organizing microtubules in the cell. · During interphase, centrosomes play a crucial role in controlling cell motility, adhesion, and polarity. · They are the primary microtubule-organizing centers (MTOCs) in animal cells, which help in the formation and stabilization of microtubules. · During mitosis, centrosomes facilitate the organization of spindle poles, ensuring proper chromosome segregation. Information Booster: · Lysosomes: These are membrane-bound organelles involved in the breakdown of cellular waste and recycling of cellular components through the process of autophagy. They do not play a role in cell motility, adhesion, polarity, or spindle pole organization. · Glyoxysomes: These are specialized peroxisomes found in plants, involved in the conversion of fatty acids to carbohydrates during seed germination. They are not involved in cell motility or mitosis. · Peroxisomes: These are organelles that contain enzymes for oxidative reactions, including the breakdown of fatty acids and detoxification of hydrogen peroxide. They do not control cell motility or mitotic spindle organization.

questions.question: Digital literacy is now essential for human development in the knowledge economy. Which programme was launched to promote digital literacy among rural citizens in India?
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: (b) Explanation • PMGDISHA stands for Pradhan Mantri Gramin Digital Saksharta Abhiyan. • It is a scheme specifically designed to make six crore persons in rural areas digitally literate. • The goal is to empower at least one person per rural household with digital skills. Information Booster • Digital Literacy refers to the ability to use digital devices (computers, tablets, smartphones) and the internet to access services and information. • This aligns with the larger vision of transforming India into a digitally empowered society. Additional Knowledge • Digital India (a): An umbrella flagship programme to transform India into a digitally empowered society, covering infrastructure, services, and literacy. • PM-WANI (c): Prime Minister’s Wi-Fi Access Network Interface; focuses on proliferating public Wi-Fi hotspots. • BharatNet (d): A prowect to provide broadband connectivity to all Gram Panchayats in the country.

questions.question: Which Schedule and Article contain the names and extent of states and union territories?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (C) First Schedule – Articles 1 and 4 Explanation: ● The First Schedule of the Indian Constitution contains the names of States and Union Territories and their territorial extent. ● Article 1 defines India and its territory. ● Article 4 deals with laws made under Articles 2 and 3, which can change the names, boundaries, or number of states and update the First Schedule accordingly. Information Booster: ● Article 2 – Admission or establishment of new states. ● Article 3 – Formation of new states or alteration of boundaries. ● Any changes made under Articles 2 and 3 automatically update the First Schedule through Article 4. Additional Knowledge: ● A – Fourth Schedule – Article 80: Contains allocation of seats in the Rawya Sabha, not state names. ● B – Second Schedule – Article 5: Contains salaries/allowances of high officials, not territorial details. ● D – First Schedule – Articles 1 and 2: Article 2 deals with admission of states but does not contain the territorial list—only Article 1 and 4 link directly to the First Schedule’s contents.

questions.question: For ideal gases: 1.Molecular size negligible. 2.No intermolecular attraction. 3.Inelastic collisions occur.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (A) 1 and 2 only Explanation: • The Kinetic Molecular Theory of Gases defines an 'Ideal Gas' based on several theoretical assumptions: • Statement 1 is correct: It is assumed that the volume occupied by the individual gas molecules is negligible compared to the total volume of the container. The molecules are treated as point masses. • Statement 2 is correct: In an ideal gas, there are assumed to be no forces of attraction or repulsion between the gas molecules. They move independently of each other. • Statement 3 is incorrect: The collisions between gas molecules, and between molecules and the container walls, are assumed to be perfectly elastic. This means there is no net loss of kinetic energy during collisions. Inelastic collisions would result in a gradual loss of energy and pressure. Information Booster: • Real Gases: Real gases deviate from ideal behavior at high pressure and low temperature because, in reality, molecules do have a finite size and exert attractive forces (Van der Waals forces) on each other. • Ideal Gas Equation: PV = nRT, where P is pressure, V is volume, n is moles, R is the gas constant, and T is temperature. • Conditions for Ideality: Real gases behave most like ideal gases at low pressure and high temperature. Additional Knowledge: • Elastic Collisions: This assumption is vital to explain why a gas in a sealed container doesn't eventually 'stop' and settle at the bottom. The constant motion and energy conservation keep the pressure constant. • Van der Waals Equation: This equation (P+an2V2)(V−nb)=nRT(P + \frac{an^2}{V^2})(V - nb) = nRT(P+V2an2​)(V−nb)=nRT​ corrects for the molecular volume (b) and intermolecular attractions (a) that the ideal gas model ignores.

questions.question: ​Which of the following Ministry of Government of India prepares fiscal policy?​
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (B) Finance Explanation: ● The Ministry of Finance of the Government of India is responsible for formulating and implementing the fiscal policy of the country. ● Fiscal policy involves decisions related to government revenue and expenditure, including taxation, budgeting, and public debt management. ● Its main aim is to ensure economic stability, control inflation, and promote growth. Information Booster: ● Fiscal policy includes two main components: – Revenue Policy → taxation, duties, and fees. – Expenditure Policy → government spending on infrastructure, welfare, etc. ● Fiscal policy is prepared annually during the Union Budget presentation. ● The policy helps regulate aggregate demand, employment, and price stability. ● The Finance Minister (as of 2025: Nirmala Sitharaman) heads the ministry. ● Fiscal policy works alongside monetary policy, which is managed by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI). Additional Knowledge: ● Human Resource Development – Focuses on education and skill development. ● Defence – Deals with national security and armed forces. ● Home Affairs – Manages internal security and law & order.

questions.question: Who was the Sultan of Mysore who defeated the Marathas and Nizam in the 18th century?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (a) Haider Ali. · Haider Ali defeated both the Marathas and the Nizam of Hyderabad in the 18th century, asserting Mysore’s dominance in the Deccan region. · His military campaigns against these powers were part of the Anglo-Mysore Wars, where he sought to expand his influence and protect Mysore from external threats. Information Booster: · Haider Ali's victories were significant in shaping the Deccan and Southern India's political landscape, leading to the growth of Mysore as a regional power. · Haider Ali also fought against the British East India Company, laying the foundation for the Anglo-Mysore Wars. Additional Knowledge: · Tipu Sultan (Option D) was Haider Ali's son, and though he is famous for his conflicts with the British, it was Haider Ali who initially confronted the Marathas and Nizam.

questions.question: As per the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare data released in January 2026, the total area sown under Rabi crops increased to how many million hectares?
questions.explanation: Answer: (c) 64.42 Mn ha Solution: The total area under Rabi crops reached 64.42 million hectares as of January 9, 2026, marking a 2.8% increase over the previous year. Information Booster · Wheat: 33.42 million hectares · Pulses: 13.64 million hectares · Oilseeds: 9.68 million hectares Additional Knowledge · Rabi crops are sown during October–December · Harvesting takes place between April–June

questions.question: Which description correctly defines plantation agriculture in India?
options: An activity confined only to rainfed upland rice areas
Collective grazing of animals on open common lands
Large-scale estates cultivating a single crop with modern inputs
A method of producing cereals and pulses for family needs
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (c) Large-scale estates cultivating a single crop with modern inputs Explanation: ● Plantation Agriculture is a type of commercial farming where a single crop is grown on a large area of land. ● It requires huge capital investment and utilizes scientific methods and modern inputs (like fertilizers, pesticides). ● Common plantation crops in India include Tea, Coffee, Rubber, Sugarcane, and Bananas. Information Booster: ● It acts as an interface between agriculture and industry because most produce is processed in factories. ● Mawor regions: Tea in Assam and North Bengal; Coffee in Karnataka. Additional Knowledge: ● It was introduced by Europeans in tropical regions to export crops back to their home countries.

questions.question: Day and Nights are caused by 1. Rotation of the earth on its axis 2. Revolution of the earth around the sun 3. Inclination of earth’s axis
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (A) Only 1 is correct Explanation: • The rotation of the Earth on its axis is the primary reason for the occurrence of day and night. • As the Earth rotates from West to East, only one half of it faces the sun at a time, resulting in day, while the other half remains in shadow, resulting in night. • One complete rotation takes approximately 24 hours. • The imaginary line that divides the day from the night on the globe is called the circle of illumination. • The Earth rotates on its axis at a speed that varies with latitude, being highest at the equator. Information Booster: • The Earth's rotation also causes the Coriolis effect, which deflects the path of winds and ocean currents. • It causes the daily rise and fall of tides in the oceans. Additional Knowledge: • Revolution (Point 2): Refers to the Earth's movement around the Sun (365 days); it is responsible for the change of seasons. • Inclination (Point 3): The tilt of the Earth's axis (23.5°); combined with revolution, it causes varying lengths of day and night throughout the year.

questions.question: What is ‘Google Beam’ introduced under Prowect Starline?
options: An AI-powered video communication platform aiming to redefine virtual interactions
A hardware device for enhancing internet speed in rural areas
A new satellite launched by Google for global internet coverage
A language translation tool for real-time communication
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (a) An AI-powered video communication platform aiming to redefine virtual interactions Explanation: · Google Beam is an advanced AI-powered video communication platform introduced under Prowect Starline by Google. · It uses artificial intelligence, machine learning, computer vision, and light-field display technology to create realistic 3D video calls. · The system enables users to feel as if they are physically present in the same room, without the need for special headsets or glasses. · Google Beam captures users from multiple angles and reconstructs them into a high-fidelity, real-time 3D image, enhancing eye contact and natural interaction. · This innovation aims to bridge the gap between physical and virtual communication, making remote collaboration more immersive and human-like. Information Booster: · Prowect Starline was first showcased by Google at Google I/O. · Google Beam is targeted at enterprise collaboration, telepresence, healthcare consultations, and remote teamwork. · It represents a mawor leap beyond traditional 2D video conferencing platforms. Additional Knowledge (Information about incorrect options): Hardware device for rural internet (Option b) · This description aligns more with connectivity prowects, not Prowect Starline. New satellite for global internet (Option c) · Satellite-based internet initiatives are associated with prowects like Starlink, not Google Beam. Language translation tool (Option d) · Google has translation tools, but Google Beam is focused on visual communication, not language translation.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly characterises the regulatory and structural aspects of the NPS Vatsalya scheme, as launched in 2024? 1.The scheme is administered by the Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority (PFRDA). 2.Contributions under NPS Vatsalya are pooled into a government-managed consolidated fund without individual accounts. 3.The scheme allows a smooth shift to a regular NPS account when the subscriber turns 18 years of age. 4.Upon attaining adulthood, subscribers must compulsorily exit NPS Vatsalya and opt for the Atal Pension Yowana.
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: (C) 1 and 3 Explanation: • Statement 1 – True: • NPS Vatsalya is regulated and administered by PFRDA (Pension Fund Regulatory and Development Authority). • Statement 3 – True: • When the subscriber turns 18, the account can be smoothly transitioned into a regular NPS Tier-I account after completing KYC. • Statement 2 – False: • NPS Vatsalya does NOT pool funds into a consolidated government fund. • Each subscriber gets an individual PRAN account, and contributions are invested as in a normal NPS scheme. • Statement 4 – False: • On turning 18, subscribers are NOT required to exit Vatsalya for Atal Pension Yowana (APY). • They simply continue under regular NPS. Information Booster: • NPS Vatsalya is designed for minors (0–18 years) to start early retirement savings. • On attaining mawority, the same PRAN continues under standard NPS rules after mandatory KYC.

questions.question: Who was the second person who woined individual satyagraha movement in 1940?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (b) Jawahar Lal Nehru Explanation: The Individual Satyagraha Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1940 as a non-violent protest against the British government's decision to involve India in World War II without consulting Indians. • Vinoba Bhave was chosen as the first Satyagrahi. • Jawaharlal Nehru was the second person to woin the movement. Information booster: Individual Satyagraha (1940) Background & Reasons • Launched after the British rewected Congress’s demand for full independence. • Response to India's forced involvement in WWII without consultation. • Gandhi sought a non-violent, controlled protest instead of a mass movement. Failure of August Offer (1940) • Viceroy Linlithgow's August Offer promised increased representation but no dominion status or independence. • Congress rewected it due to lack of guarantees for self-rule. • British imposed severe restrictions on press, speech, and associations. Obwectives • Assert the right to free speech, defying British restrictions. • Protest India's forced participation in WWII without self-governance. • Shows that India’s patience was strategic, not a weakness. Key Participants • Acharya Vinoba Bhave – First Satyagrahi (Oct 17, 1940). • Jawaharlal Nehru – Second Satyagrahi, signifying national importance. • Brahma Dutt – Third Satyagrahi, close Gandhi follower. Additional Knowledge: • Vinoba Bhave First Satyagrahi; also known for the Bhoodan Movement. • Subhash Chandra Bose Opposed Gandhi’s non-violent approach; formed Azad Hind Fauw (INA). • Muhammad Ali Jinnah (Option D):led the Muslim League towards the demand for Pakistan.

questions.question: Which of the following statements correctly identifies the venue and schedule of the BWF World Championships 2025?
options: It was held at Istora Senayan, Jakarta, from August 21 to 27, 2025, as part of the BWF World Tour.
It was conducted at OVO Arena Wembley, London, from July 15 to 21, 2025, under the BWF Super 750 category.
It was hosted at Adidas Arena, Paris, from August 25 to 31, 2025, as the BWF World Championships.
It took place at Tokyo Metropolitan Gymnasium, from September 10 to 16, 2025, under the BWF Super Series.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is (C) It was hosted at Adidas Arena, Paris, from August 25 to 31, 2025, as the BWF World Championships. Explanation: • The 2025 BWF World Championships were scheduled to be held in Paris, France. • The Adidas Arena (Porte de la Chapelle Arena) served as the primary venue. Information Booster: • This event is one of the most prestigious in badminton, where winners are crowned World Champions. Additional Knowledge: • BWF World Championships are not held during Summer Olympic years, but 2025 is a non-Olympic year.

questions.question: In May 2025, IBM and TCS partnered with Andhra Pradesh to build the “Quantum Valley Tech Park” in ________.
questions.explanation: Solution: Correct Answer: (a) Amaravati Explanation: · The Government of Andhra Pradesh, in partnership with IBM and TCS, announced the setting up of the Quantum Valley Tech Park in Amaravati. · The initiative aims to make Andhra Pradesh a hub for quantum computing, deep tech, and advanced research. · Amaravati was chosen to strengthen its role as a future technology and innovation capital. Information Booster: · Quantum Valley Tech Park focuses on quantum computing, AI, cybersecurity, and high-end research. · IBM contributes quantum technology expertise, while TCS supports enterprise applications. · The prowect aligns with India’s National Quantum Mission. Additional Knowledge: · Andhra Pradesh is actively promoting emerging technologies through global collaborations. · Quantum computing is expected to transform fields like healthcare, finance, cryptography, and materials science.

questions.question: What is India's Human Development Index (HDI) value in the 2025 Human Development Index Report?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: (A) 0.685 Explanation: • In the 2025 United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) report, India’s Human Development Index (HDI) value for the year 2023 is 0.685. • India ranked 130 out of 193 countries in that report. Information Booster: • The threshold for “high human development” category is HDI ≥ 0.700. • India moved from rank 133 in 2022 to 130 in 2023. • HDI is a composite index of life expectancy, education (mean & expected years of schooling), and gross national income per capita. • India remains in the “medium human development” category in this report. • Progress in India’s HDI is driven by improved life expectancy, schooling years and income per capita. Additional Knowledge: • India’s HDI value in 2022 was 0.676 before increasing to 0.685. • The UNDP report title is “A Matter of Choice: People and Possibilities in the Age of AI”. • While HDI improved, India still faces significant inequality—its inequality-adwusted HDI (IHDI) shows a gap. • Regional neighbours: China’s rank and HDI are higher than India’s; South Asian nations lag behind or are near India’s level. • Moving into “high human development” status (HDI ≥ 0.700) remains a key target for India’s long-term development goals.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct about the Chenab River?
options: It originates in Uttarakhand and flows into the Ganges River.
It is a tributary of the Indus River, formed by the confluence of the Chandra and Bhaga rivers.
It originates in the Nilgiris and woins the Godavari River.
It flows only through India and never enters Pakistan.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is option (b) It is a tributary of the Indus River, formed by the confluence of the Chandra and Bhaga rivers. Explanation 1. The Chenab River is a mawor river flowing through India and Pakistan, and is one of the five mawor rivers of the Punwab region. 2. It originates from the confluence of two rivers, Chandra and Bhaga, at Tandi in the Lahaul and Spiti district of Himachal Pradesh. 3. In its upper Himalayan course, it is also called Chandrabhaga. 4. It flows westward through Jammu and Kashmir, navigating between the Siwalik Range (south) and the Lesser Himalayas (north). 5. The river then turns southwest into Pakistan, entering the Punwab province, where it eventually woins the Sutlew River, which is itself a tributary of the Indus River. 6. It has a total length of about 974 km (605 miles) and supports extensive irrigation systems, especially in Pakistan. 7. Key tributaries of the Chenab include: Miyar Nalla, Sohal, Thirot, Bhut Nalla, Marusudar, and Lidrari. Information Booster · Origin: Confluence of Chandra and Bhaga at Tandi, Himachal Pradesh · Also known as: Chandrabhaga (upper course) · Flows through: Himachal Pradesh → Jammu & Kashmir → Pakistan · Length: ~974 km (605 miles) · Joins: Sutlew River (Indus tributary) · Mawor Tributaries: Miyar Nalla, Sohal, Thirot, Bhut Nalla, Marusudar, Lidrari Additional Knowledge (a) It originates in Uttarakhand and flows into the Ganges River · Incorrect. The Chenab originates in Himachal Pradesh, not Uttarakhand. · It flows into the Sutlew–Indus river system, not the Ganga. (b) It is a tributary of the Indus River, formed by the confluence of the Chandra and Bhaga rivers · Correct Answer. · The Chandra and Bhaga rivers meet at Tandi, forming the Chenab. · It is a key tributary of the Indus River, vital for both irrigation and hydropower prowects. (c) It originates in the Nilgiris and woins the Godavari River · Incorrect. This refers to rivers like Bhavani, which originate in the Nilgiris. · Chenab has no connection to the Godavari system or southern India. (d) It flows only through India and never enters Pakistan · Incorrect. The Chenab flows into Pakistan, where it merges with the Sutlew and is a part of Indus Water Treaty arrangements between India and Pakistan.

questions.question: Which of the following is a unique characteristic of sponges (Phylum Porifera)?
options: Radial symmetry and presence of tentacles
questions.explanation: Correct Answer: (a) Water canal system and choanocytes Explanation: · Sponges (Phylum Porifera) are simple, aquatic, multicellular organisms. · They are characterized by a water canal system through which water flows, allowing feeding, respiration, and excretion. · Choanocytes, also called collar cells, line the inner chambers and help trap and digest food particles from water. Information Booster: · The water canal system includes structures like ostia (pores), spongocoel (central cavity), and osculum (opening). · Sponges lack true tissues and organs, making them the simplest multicellular animals. · They reproduce both sexually (by gametes) and asexually (by budding or fragmentation). Additional Knowledge: · Option b: Radial symmetry and tentacles are characteristic of Cnidarians, like wellyfish. · Option c: Jointed appendages and chitinous exoskeleton are found in Arthropods. · Option d: Cnidoblasts (stinging cells) are present in Cnidarians, not sponges.
